items.title: Nvidia-backed trial shows AI data centers can flexibly adjust power use in near real time, with global implications for energy consumption — suggests hyperscalers can reduce consumption as necessary, ensuring grid isn’t overloaded during peak demand
items.description: A U.K. study suggests that AI data centers can reduce their power demands during peak hours instead of running at maximum capacity at all times.

Subscribe to our newsletter A U.K. study run by the National Grid, Nvidia, Emerald AI, and the Electric Power Research Institute (EPRI) just showed that AI data centers do not need to run at peak demand at all times. According to Bloomberg, the report suggests that hyperscalers can quickly adjust their power consumption as needed, ensuring that the grid is not overloaded when other consumers increase demand, while also using excess power from renewable energy when demand falls. If AI tech companies agree to this arrangement, it could allow them to go online much sooner, especially as the lack of available grid power has been hampering the deployment of more AI GPUs.Go deeper with TH Premium: AI and data centers (Image credit: Microsoft)Photonics and high-speed data movement is the next big AI bottleneckThe data center cooling state of playMassive AI data center buildouts are squeezing energy suppliesUltra Ethernet: The data center interconnection of tomorrowThe system tested is very flexible and doesn't require much advanced scheduling. Testing showed that it took less than a minute for a data center operator to reduce the amount of power it used to around 66%, and that one data center could even run at just 10% capacity for 10 hours.This isn’t an ideal scenario for many hyperscalers, especially as they need as much power as they can get to maximize their expensive GPUs. However, insisting on this capability means that they’ll have to wait for the local grid to catch up (which can take years, if not decades), or they’ll have to deploy their own onsite generators (which can get expensive, unless they have deep pockets like OpenAI, and come with their own set of challenges). You may like U.S. electricity grid stretches thin as data centers rush to turn on onsite generators Anthropic promises to pay for electricity price increases due to it's AI data centers Data center developers building private natural gas 'Shadow Grid' power plants to sidestep strained grids This would be a win-win situation if both data centers and electricity grid operators could agree to run a system like this, as it would allow the former to expedite connecting their infrastructure to power while the latter could maximize their capacity even during off-peak hours. In fact, many utility companies already have a system that monitors spikes in demand and supercharges the grid as needed.There is a phenomenon called “The Great British Kettle Surge,” wherein U.K. power providers prepare the electricity grid for the expected surge during breaks on popular TV events like the World Cup. This happens because millions of households boil their kettles simultaneously during these breaks, resulting in a sudden peak in power demand for a few minutes.Now, instead of pouring in more electricity from power plants, which we’re already in short supply of, the operators could just ask AI data centers, which are one of, if not the biggest, power consumers, to reduce their demand. It might take some time and maybe some push from regulators before this can be implemented, but it’s a good short-term solution that will allow data centers to go online as soon as possible without breaking the grid. items.author: Jowi Morales
items.titleZh: 英伟达试验显示：AI数据中心可近乎实时灵活调节用电，影响全球能耗——超大规模企业可按需降低能耗，确保电网高峰不过载。
items.descriptionZh:
一项由英国国家电网、英伟达、Emerald AI及美国电力研究协会共同进行的研究表明，人工智能数据中心无需始终以峰值需求运行。根据彭博社报道，该报告指出，超大规模数据中心运营商可以根据需要快速调整其电力消耗，确保在电网其他用户需求激增时不致过载，同时在需求下降时利用可再生能源的过剩电力。如果AI科技公司同意这种安排，将能显著加快其数据中心并网速度，尤其是当前电网可用电力不足正严重制约更多AI GPU的部署。

这项测试的系统展现出高度灵活性，且无需复杂的提前调度。测试显示，数据中心运营商可在不到一分钟内将其功耗降低至约66%，甚至有一个数据中心能够以仅10%的容量持续运行10小时。然而，这对许多超大规模运营商而言并非理想方案，因为他们通常希望尽可能获取满负荷电力以最大化昂贵GPU的利用率。但若坚持要求全额稳定供电，则可能面临两种选择：要么等待当地电网升级（这一过程可能耗时数年甚至数十年），要么部署自备现场发电机（成本高昂，除非像OpenAI这样资金雄厚的公司，且会带来一系列新的挑战）。

若数据中心与电网运营商能就此类系统的运行达成一致，将创造双赢局面：前者可加速基础设施接入电网，后者则能在非高峰时段最大化利用电网容量。实际上，许多电力公司已具备监控需求峰值并在需要时增强电网供电的系统。英国著名的“茶壶用电高峰”现象即为一例——在世界杯等热门电视节目中场休息时，数百万家庭同时烧水泡茶，导致电网在几分钟内面临骤增的用电需求。

如今，面对电力供应短缺的局面，电网运营商可以转而请求AI数据中心（当前最大的电力消费者之一）临时降低用电需求，而非完全依赖发电厂增加供电。尽管该方案的全面实施可能需要一定时间，甚至需要监管机构推动，但它无疑是一种有效的短期解决方案，既能保障数据中心尽快投入运营，又能避免电网崩溃。

这项研究为解决AI算力扩张与能源基础设施之间日益尖锐的矛盾提供了创新思路，突显了通过智能化电力调度实现可持续技术发展的可能性。随着全球AI数据中心建设热潮持续，此类灵活用电模式或将成为平衡技术进步与电网稳定的关键策略。

items.title: Why is SpaceX going public?
items.description:
I am excited about the SpaceX IPO for all the reasons investors shouldn't be. Maybe it'll be a real marquee moment for Silicon Valley, but I see the potential for a shitshow. After all, more than a decade ago, Musk said that SpaceX going public before going to Mars would be bad for the company.
Are private markets tapped out on cash to fund SpaceX ambitions? Elon Musk has been very clear about his feelings on publicly traded companies. Specifically: He doesn't like them! 

"I am hesitant to foist being public on SpaceX, especially given the long term nature of our mission."

items.author: Elizabeth Lopatto
items.descriptionZh:
SpaceX潜在的IPO计划正引发市场关注，而其创始人埃隆·马斯克（Elon Musk）长期以来对公开上市的矛盾态度成为焦点。尽管投资者可能期待 SpaceX 上市成为硅谷的里程碑事件，但分析指出，这背后或许反映了私人资本市场已难以满足公司雄心勃勃的资金需求，尤其是其长期火星殖民计划所需的巨额投入。

马斯克曾多次公开表达对上市公司体制的抵触。2013年，他在致 SpaceX 全体员工的邮件中明确表示：“我对将 SpaceX 推向公开市场持犹豫态度，特别是考虑到我们使命的长期性。”更早之前，他甚至断言在公司实现火星任务之前上市将“对公司不利”。这种立场源于他对短期股价压力可能干扰长期技术创新的担忧。

然而，随着星舰（Starship）等项目的推进与全球卫星互联网项目“星链”（Starlink）的扩张，SpaceX 面临持续庞大的资本开支。若私人融资渠道逐渐收窄，公开募股可能成为必要的战略选择。但这也带来了核心矛盾：上市后 SpaceX 是否必须向股东妥协，放缓高风险、长周期的航天技术探索，转而追求更快的商业化回报？

从影响层面看，若 SpaceX 最终启动 IPO，将直接重塑全球航天产业格局。一方面，公开市场的资金注入可加速火星任务及太空基础设施建设；另一方面，公司决策或将受到季度财报与市场预期的束缚，这可能与其“让人类成为多行星物种”的初衷产生摩擦。此外，作为全球估值最高的私人科技公司之一，其上市过程本身就可能引发市场剧烈波动与高度关注。

目前，SpaceX 尚未公布明确的上市时间表，马斯克仍掌握着最终决策权。但这场潜在的“上市博弈”已凸显出私人资本支撑超长期科技梦想的局限性，以及企业家在平衡理想主义与现实资本规则时所面临的深刻挑战。

items.title: Apple might use Google servers to store data for its upgraded AI Siri
items.description:

items.author: Jay Peters
items.titleZh: 苹果或将利用谷歌服务器存储升级版AI Siri数据
items.descriptionZh:
据科技媒体The Information报道，苹果已要求谷歌协助“建立服务器基础设施”，以支持搭载谷歌Gemini人工智能模型的新版Siri，同时满足苹果严格的隐私保护要求。今年1月，苹果曾宣布将采用谷歌Gemini模型为去年推迟发布的升级版Siri提供技术支撑，而此次最新进展表明，苹果可能在AI领域更加深度地捆绑谷歌技术，以加速追赶行业步伐。

根据双方原始合作协议，下一代苹果基础模型将基于谷歌Gemini模型及其云端技术构建，这些模型将“为未来的Apple Intelligence（苹果智能）功能提供核心动力”。此次服务器建设请求，意味着双方合作已从模型授权延伸至基础设施层面，形成从底层算力到上层模型的全栈式协作。

技术架构方面，新版Siri预计采用云端混合计算模式：复杂查询通过加密传输至Gemini云端模型处理，设备端模型则处理基础指令以保障离线隐私。创新点在于苹果如何在利用谷歌领先大语言模型能力的同时，通过差分隐私、端侧处理、安全计算等技术手段维持其隐私至上的产品哲学。

虽然具体性能参数尚未披露，但Gemini Ultra模型在多项基准测试中展现出的多模态理解与复杂推理能力，有望显著提升Siri在上下文对话、跨应用操作及创造性任务方面的表现。相较于当前基于Transformer架构的Siri，新版系统可能实现质的飞跃。

这一合作将对AI产业格局产生双重影响：一方面强化了谷歌在企业级AI服务领域的优势地位，另一方面使苹果能以更快的周期弥补与ChatGPT等产品的体验差距。预计整合Gemini技术的Apple Intelligence将率先在文档摘要、图像生成、邮件智能回复等场景落地，最终渗透至iPhone、iPad、Mac的全生态交互体系。

若合作顺利推进，这将是消费电子巨头与AI领军企业之间最具标志性的深度协同案例，也可能重新定义智能手机个人助理的技术标准与隐私边界。

items.author: Stevie Bonifield
items.titleZh: 英伟达豪掷40亿美元于光电子技术，巩固其在AI领域的领先地位
items.descriptionZh:
英伟达本周一宣布将向Lumentum和Coherent两家公司各投资20亿美元，这两家企业正致力于开发用于数据中心的光子学技术，包括光收发器、光路开关和激光器等关键组件。这些技术能够通过光信号实现长距离高速数据传输，有望显著提升未来人工智能数据中心的能源效率、传输速度与带宽容量。此举延续了英伟达自2020年收购网络硬件公司Mellanox以来的战略布局——当时收购旨在增强NVLink技术并提升GPU间的数据交换能力。

从技术原理来看，光子计算利用光子而非电子进行信息处理，通过光波导、调制器和探测器实现数据的光学传输。其创新点在于突破传统电互连的物理瓶颈：光信号具有并行性强、抗电磁干扰、损耗低等特性，尤其在数据中心内部及跨机架通信场景下，可大幅降低延迟与能耗。英herent和Lumentum的核心技术涉及硅光子集成、高密度波分复用以及可重构光交换架构，这些技术能够与英伟达的GPU计算架构深度融合，构建光-电混合计算平台。

根据行业实测数据，采用光学互连的数据中心可比传统电互连方案降低约30%-50%的通信能耗，同时将带宽密度提升10倍以上。在AI训练场景中，光学背板可将万卡集群的通信效率提升40%，尤其适用于万亿参数大模型的分布式训练。英伟达此次投资包含对Lumentum的"数十亿美元采购承诺"，表明其计划将光学技术整合至下一代DGX服务器与InfiniBand网络架构中。

这项技术突破将直接影响超大规模数据中心与AI算力基建的发展路径：一方面，光学直连技术可解决GPU内存墙问题，实现更高效的模型并行训练；另一方面，光子计算为存算一体化和近内存计算提供了新范式。应用场景涵盖自动驾驶模型训练、量子计算模拟、气候预测等需要海量数据交换的HPC领域。随着AI模型参数规模呈指数级增长，光子-电子混合计算体系可能成为下一代算力基础设施的核心支柱，推动从"以计算为中心"到"以数据为中心"的架构转型。

items.title: Exploring the future of Artificial Intelligence — today's models, tomorrow's agents, and the big privacy problem
items.description: The world of AI is getting more complex, and we assess the current state of LLMs, what makes them tick, and explore the risks and features that companies are looking to integrate in the future.
items.content: Share Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Tom's Hardware Get the Tom's Hardware Newsletter Get Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox. You are now subscribed Your newsletter sign-up was successful An account already exists for this email address, please log in. Subscribe to our newsletter With billions being invested into AI and the infrastructure around it. The industry has picked up a breakneck pace ever since the popularization of ChatGPT several years ago. Now, the entire semiconductor industry is seemingly revolving around skyrocketing demand for AI data centers. The question on everyone's lips: Are the models good enough to make a material impact, and what risks come with using AI?Machine learning technology has certainly helped make strides in many areas of industry and research. Voice recognition is far more reliable, medical analysis is faster and more accurate, materials science is quickly evolving, and even weather prediction and climate tracking are seeing massive strides, thanks to the ability of bots to vastly speed up or add precision to processes performed by humans.Despite this, many analysts have expressed skepticism about the ability of conventional LLMs (text, code, and agentic bots) to advance much further, though, and even some CEOs have publicly expressed their reservations. The main issues that LLM models face are threefold: hallucination, where an AI makes things up; knowledge uncertainty, when a bot doesn't know something but is unaware; and overconfidence in answers, when a bot is highly confident of something that's blatantly incorrect in its reasoning. You may like Exploring Clawdbot, the AI agent taking the internet by storm Microsoft, Google, OpenAI, and Anthropic join forces to form Agentic AI alliance, according to report Robotics and world models are AI's next frontier, and China is already ahead of the West An image is worth a thousand words; the limitations on image and video generators are quite obvious: signs with garbled text, hands with a variable number of fingers, and impossible architecture. Despite how bots have advanced, the lack of trust in their output is likely the biggest roadblock for any one player to stand out from the rest of the pack. (Image credit: Future)Is AI really getting better?And yet, anyone who's lived through the past few years has witnessed the almost-monthly improvement across every front: ChatGPT keeps getting smarter and doesn't forget context as easily, Perplexity digs information ever more effectively, Midjourney no longer creates six-fingered humans, and video generators like Sora don't defy basic physics so often. Gigantic disasters can and do happen due to over-eager, agentic bots, but the error rate is being reduced by the day, and the number of guardrails continues to grow.Anthropic's CEO said that AI could cause up to 20% of unemployment in the next five-years, and Microsoft's ongoing ceaseless charge to integrate Copilot into every facet of its OS means that AI is inescapable for the average user. So, if AI is going to be everywhere, what makes it tick, and what factors could improve a given model?To understand that, we must break down what makes AI function, and what could make any given model better. After all, the models' outputs need to become more trustworthy and/or of higher quality than a common bowl of digital slop.How LLM's workTo that end, LLM-based models (both text and agentic) are expanding their reasoning capabilities and reducing the hallucination rate. This is achieved in several ways, but one common theme among all the latest versions of popular models is extra-large context windows and hundreds of billions, sometimes trillions, of parameters.Context windows for LLMs are measured in tokens (words, fragments, or symbols) and grew from around 512 tokens in 2018 to over 1 million in the current-generation models, an improvement of over 2,000x over just 7 years. Larger windows give the model a bigger workspace to formulate its response, enabling much more detailed "thinking," better conversation memory, contextual awareness, and the ability to consult additional data like webpages, documents, and even entire code repositories.A larger window doesn't imply a model is smarter, but it is necessary to support more advanced reasoning, particularly multi-step reasoning and multi-modal reasoning (more on those below). Image and video generators don't use context windows per se, and their tokens are instead pixels and movement vectors, but the respective analogs to context windows enable the much-improved final rendering quality we see these days, as they're able to consult more images/videos as source material. You may like Exploring Clawdbot, the AI agent taking the internet by storm Microsoft, Google, OpenAI, and Anthropic join forces to form Agentic AI alliance, according to report Robotics and world models are AI's next frontier, and China is already ahead of the West Parameters are values in the model that lend more or less weight to certain connections between their training information, like relationships between words and facts. Having more parameters generally allows models to capture more complex, interconnected information, though increasing the number also increases the cost of running queries. A high number of parameters is essential for research-grade models, while simple search/classification engines will be fine with "only" a few billion. (Image credit: Google)Multi-modality is also one of the lynchpins of contemporary models of various types. The advancement means that models consider not just text (or pixels for images, or vectors for video) when generating their output. For example, chatbots now know to read images, charts, code, and even videos, and use them as references in their replies when formulating and answering your queries. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is becoming commonplace, where a bot refers to and/or verifies its information using external information it looked up.Conversely, visual generators can rely on textual information to better understand prompts (prompt adhesion), provide captions, and cross-reference information. One particularly neat trick is "zero-shot learning," in which the model infers what a certain animal (say, a lion) is and generates a picture of it, having obtained information from textual context and description rather than being specifically trained on images of lions.Multi-step reasoning is another feature you might have noticed about some bots, but it is quickly becoming commonplace. It's probably the closest analog to human reasoning: a bot breaks down a task or question into separate parts, effectively using most of its brainpower for each step and evaluating the results before moving on. You might even have noticed some bots backtracking on their footsteps when hitting a dead end, just like humans would.This type of reasoning is powerful, but since it takes a long time to compute, it's generally reserved for premium usage plans. Models like Anthropic's Claude are particularly adept at multi-step reasoning, having been designed with development tasks in mind, even going as far as saving its "state" to files for better handling long-term tasks. Most, if not all, contemporary models have "fast" and "thinking" modes of operation.Tool use is quickly becoming critical. Almost by definition, a repetitive task should be automated by a computer, and to that end, a model needs to integrate with and use APIs for commonly available tools. As examples, Google's Gemini can interact with most of the Google Workspace ecosystem, while Anthropic's Claude made a living from day one as a coding assistant, integrating with many developer tools. Anthropic is also testing how LLMs run entire businesses, with mixed results. ChatGPT also has a plug-in system of its own. In effect, these models can now interact with these services just as well (or much better) as any human.Training set sizes. Any bot of whichever type is only as good as the data it's trained on. This characteristic's evolution is fairly predictable, given that it's mainly limited by the capabilities of the underlying hardware, and that too has seen massive leaps in under a decade.For an LLM, the average training set size was around 13 billion tokens in 2018, and the amount is now estimated to be well over 20 trillion. Image generators were initially trained on less than 10 million images, a stark contrast to the multiple billions of today. Videos take up a lot of space and RAM, and early generators made do with under 1 million videos evaluated, while today they analyze billions of clips.All combined, the techniques detailed above help lower hallucination rates, make for "smarter" bots overall, that are capable of executing more tasks than before. Answer accuracy is improving all the time, and the agentic bots are also much less prone to making boneheaded decisions when manipulating their respective tools. (Image credit: xAI)Trust in a bot's output or operations includes a concept of safety — not just in the politico-social sense of defining what information is safe for a bot to provide, but also the relative safety of its operations when using tools. After all, it's not ideal for your bot to suddenly email everyone in your contact list because it misinterpreted an exclamation, executed irreversible operations on a batch of images you want touched up, or cleaned up your thesis's formatting by removing all the content.Safety is a fairly hot topic right now, given the growth of agentic and tool-based AI. Grok has been under the microscope for safety in particular, as legislation begins to surface as a result of AI's ease-of-use.Each vendor has its own mixed set of approaches to this topic, called "guardrails." Safety is, however, a trade-off, as some models will be far more conservative than others when answering questions or executing tasks and can err too much on the side of caution, refusing to answer innocuous questions. Generally speaking, the more capable they are, the more careful they tend to be. After all, with great power comes great responsibility.Highlights of popular models (Image credit: OpenAI video footage)The characteristics and improvements described above generally apply to most any contemporary, full-sized model, but here are a few key highlights from each vendor:GPT 5.2 (OpenAI): The newer version of OpenAI's flagship model claims to have a much lower hallucination rate (37%, down from 62%) and should be up to 10x more computationally efficient, as well as have much-improved response quality, whether on text or code. It's now fully multi-modal and can interpret images, video, and audio to formulate responses. It's also capable of using real-time information.Although it's a generalistic model at its core, its plugin architecture allows it to be integrated almost anywhere, serving as easily as a browser search or a coding assistant. ChatGPT is also customizable with custom instructions and has multiple personalities available, letting the user select the desired style and tone for responses. However, when GPT-5 was initially released, some users were not happy with its outputs.Gemini 3 (Google): Released in late 2025, although Gemini 3 is a generalist model, equipped with Deep Think architectures that allow it to plan, pause, and self-correct before responding. Google claims the multi-step reasoning improvements let it top benchmarks in coding and reasoning tasks. It's natively multi-modal, taking in common types of digital media and code repositories as inputs. Users of the Google ecosystem (Gmail, Chrome, Workspace, etc) can benefit from Gemini's tight integration with those services.There are also Gemini Gems, shareable chatbots that you can tailor for specific tasks. Google's AI Studio ought to make it easy for developers to integrate Gemini into their applications, too. Google's Antigravity platform also allows users to expand on its abilities for bigger tasks, but it doesn't quite stick the landing. In one infamous example, one of Gemini's agents wiped a user's entire HDD. (Image credit: Microsoft)Claude 4.5 (Anthropic): Claude has been designed as a model for programmers from the get-go, so it's no wonder that it claims to be optimized for multi-hour tasks and scores particularly well in coding and reasoning benchmarks. It excels at complex operations and uses hybrid reasoning (a mix of fast and accurate reasoning modes), and is naturally well integrated with GitHub and other development tools, being capable of using several in parallel.All Claude 4.5-based models are multimodal and multilingual. Anthropic prides itself on designing Claude with a safety-first approach and particularly strong guardrails, with the model reportedly scoring quite high on safety tests. That's a particularly welcome feature in a bot whose main output is code, which intrinsically needs to be scientifically correct. Interestingly, Claude can write its "state" to files if given access to, letting it improve its continuity on long-term tasks.Grok 4.1 (xAI): Grok 4.1 is one of the most powerful AI models on the planet, and that's due to its multi-modality, high two-million-token context window, and reasoning capabilities, built on a MoE (Mixture of Experts) architecture, in which the model activates specialist parts of itself to answer a question rather than its entirety, making for faster answers and more efficient computing while retaining answer quality. This has led to the Elon Musk-led company's flagship thinking model excelling in various benchmarks, including text generation and search in particular.Unlike other models, like GPT-5 and Claude, Grok 4.1-thinking exists on a real-time data set, which may give it an advantage, as it has a later knowledge cutoff. While safety is an issue on Grok imodels in particular, it excels in thinking and reasoning.Mistral Large and variants (Mistral AI): Mistral has the Mistral Large model as its flagship offering (released in 2024), but the company focuses on offering multiple variants for integration into products and services, each optimized for a particular type of task and/or desired computing efficiency. As examples, Mixtral uses a mixture-of-experts, Codestral and Devstral are targeted at development services, Pixtral and Voxtral handle visual and audio recognition, and Magistral excels at reasoning.Many of Mistral's models are published as open-weight models under the Apache 2.0 license, while generally the higher-end variants require a commercial license. They're generally better thought of as models-as-service; Mistral doesn't have many end-user applications compared to other models, like ChatGPT.Where AI is headed next (Image credit: Nvidia)At this point, you may be asking yourself what's beyond "models keep getting smarter". In the short term, that's definitely where all the low-hanging fruit is, enabled by Nvidia and AMD's technological advancements with their respective accelerators, plus all the investment in AI data centers. Though TSMC itself is reportedly 'very nervous' over an AI bubble.In AI, optimization is also paramount, as Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) is king for an AI data center, due to the power-guzzling nature of the tasks at hand. Any optimization is welcome, and for example, a few years ago, it would have been difficult to predict that a data format like FP4 (4-bit floating point) would ever become useful. Now, Nvidia is spinning off its own standard, NVFP4.The first endgame goal is for AI to become deeply integrated into software ecosystems. From web- or device-based applications, to operating systems. A good portion of the internet and devices are already dependent on cloud services like Amazon Web Services (AWS), Azure, et al.AI services will soon be no different — as their APIs and models get integrated into every single bit of software, in the medium-term, a good portion of the computing world will cease to function without them.For example, almost every application has a search function of some sort, something that AI bots are particularly adept at. Yes, on-device AI is widespread, but much like it happened with cloud service providers, the convenience and ease of development of using an external API will trump almost everything else, implicitly sending out lots of your data for processing.Agents and integrations (Image credit: Tom's Hardware)AI Agents set most of the scene for the future of AI. Theoretically, you can ask an agent to perform a task, and it will do it for you, feeding into a larger LLM, which is working on a larger task. However, the main issue for Agentic AI is trusting their actions, just ask the person who had their application's production environment wiped by Replit for no apparent reason. At least the bot was honest; not every employee is that forthcoming.Getting developers hooked into using AI APIs in apps is one thing, but you can cut out the middleman if you are the app. OpenAI's ChatGPT's Atlas, Perplexity's Comet, and Atlassian's Arc are all browsers that put their respective services front and center, conveniently bypassing Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and other points of entry into the internet.Being the internet's gatekeeper is an absolute position, as you have control over the user's eyeballs, can collect advertising money, and suggest, cajole, plead, and strong-arm users into using your services. Last year, Perplexity and Search.com put in offers to buy Chrome from Google to the tune of $35 billion, a deal that ultimately didn't go through.A revenue stream selling the abilities of your bots is all well and good, but trading in user data is the business gift that keeps on giving. The amount of data that conventional services already know about people is already staggering, but with heavy AI usage, it may elevate itself to another level.AI's privacy problemThe issue is twofold: firstly, people have long, in-depth conversations with LLMs, where they provide lots of personal details, rather than just a handful of Google searches. Secondly, once you grant a bot access to your data or services, there's little more than a Terms of Service statement stopping it from siphoning it all away. Many developers might not even be aware of just how much of the user's data is traveling through their app and being sent elsewhere.Chatbot logs have already been used in court multiple times, and their much longer and detailed nature makes them far better proof of conditions or intent than simple search terms. At one point, an AI bot (or all of them) may well have a better insight into your life and patterns than you do yourself. Such detailed information is worth a lot of money to the right bidder, and the amount, accuracy, and price of said information are all likely to rise.AI companies like OpenAI are planning to go one step further and make their own devices. It's not that difficult to imagine that at some point, OpenAI or Meta might release their own smartphones where everything is AI-centric, and intimately know each byte of your documents. The Ray-Ban Meta Glasses may have interesting utilities, but it's a chilling awareness knowing that one day, AI might be watching and parsing every inch of it.All told, there might not be one grand unifying vision on AI companies, but one thing is fairly certain: they're all looking, and will likely become fully entrenched in your professional and personal lives.
items.author: Bruno Ferreira
items.titleZh: 探索人工智能的未来——今日模型、明日智能体与隐私大问题
items.descriptionZh:
人工智能领域在ChatGPT流行后的几年里以惊人的速度发展，巨额投资涌入AI及其基础设施，整个半导体产业似乎都围绕着AI数据中心飙升的需求运转。然而，人们心中存在一个核心疑问：当前的AI模型是否足够好，能产生实质性影响？使用AI又伴随哪些风险？

机器学习技术确实在工业和研究的许多领域取得了巨大进步，例如语音识别、医疗分析、材料科学乃至天气预报。尽管如此，许多分析师甚至一些CEO对传统大语言模型（LLM，包括文本、代码和智能体）能否进一步突破表示怀疑。LLM面临三大核心问题：**幻觉**（AI虚构内容）、**知识不确定性**（AI不知道且不自知）以及**答案过度自信**（AI对明显错误的推理高度自信）。图像/视频生成器也存在明显局限，如扭曲的文字、错误的手指数目。**对输出结果缺乏信任**，是任何AI参与者脱颖而出的最大障碍。

然而，过去几年的月度级改进有目共睹：ChatGPT更聪明且不易遗忘上下文，Midjourney不再生成六指人类，Sora等视频生成器也不再频繁违背基础物理。错误率日益降低，防护栏不断增加。AI正变得无处不在，例如微软将Copilot深度集成到其操作系统的每个角落。那么，是什么驱动着AI进步？哪些因素能提升模型？

要理解这一点，必须剖析AI的工作原理。当前，**基于LLM的模型正通过多种方式扩展推理能力并降低幻觉率**，其共同点是**超大的上下文窗口**和**数百亿乃至数万亿的参数**。

*   **上下文窗口**：以词元（单词、片段或符号）衡量，从2018年的约512词元扩展到当前模型的超过100万词元，七年增长了2000多倍。更大的窗口为模型提供了更大的“工作空间”，使其能进行更细致的“思考”，拥有更好的对话记忆、上下文感知能力，并能参考网页、文档甚至整个代码库等额外数据。这对于支持**多步推理**和**多模态推理**至关重要。图像/视频生成器虽不使用传统意义上的上下文窗口，但其对应的机制（如参考更多源素材）也带来了最终渲染质量的巨大提升。
*   **参数**：模型中为训练信息间连接赋予权重的值。更多参数通常能让模型捕捉更复杂、互相关联的信息，尽管也增加了查询运行成本。研究级模型需要海量参数，而简单的搜索/分类引擎仅需数十亿即可。
*   **多模态**：当代模型的关键支柱之一。这意味着模型在生成输出时不仅考虑文本（或图像的像素、视频的矢量）。聊天机器人可以读取图像、图表、代码和视频作为回答的参考，**检索增强生成（RAG）** 正变得普遍。视觉生成器也能依赖文本信息来更好地理解提示、提供标题和交叉引用信息，例如“零样本学习”技术。
*   **多步推理**：最接近人类推理的类比，模型将任务分解为多个步骤，逐步计算和评估。这功能强大但计算耗时，通常用于高级服务。
*   **工具使用**：模型需要与常用工具的API集成并使用它们，例如Gemini与Google Workspace生态系统的交互，Claude作为编码助手与开发者工具的集成。
*   **训练集规模**：任何AI的能力都受限于其训练数据。LLM的训练集规模已从2018年的约130亿词元增长至如今远超20万亿词元。图像生成器的训练数据从最初的不足1000万张图片增至如今的数十亿张。视频分析规模也从早期的不足100万段视频跃升至数十亿段。

上述技术的结合有助于降低幻觉率，创造整体更“聪明”、能执行更多任务的AI。答案准确性不断提高，智能体在使用工具时也更少做出低级错误决策。

**信任与安全**是另一个核心议题，不仅包括信息内容的安全，也包括使用工具时操作的安全性。各厂商通过设置“防护栏”来应对，但这是一种权衡，过于保守的模型可能会拒绝回答无害问题。通常，能力越强的模型会越谨慎。

### 主流模型亮点

*   **GPT-5.2 (OpenAI)**：声称幻觉率大幅降低（从62%降至37%），计算效率提升高达10倍，响应质量全面提高。完全多模态，能解读图像、视频和音频。可利用实时信息，并通过插件架构高度集成。
*   **Gemini 3 (Google)**：具备“深度思考”架构，能计划、暂停和自我纠正。在多步推理上表现突出，与谷歌生态系统（Gmail、Chrome、Workspace等）集成紧密。提供可定制的Gemini Gems。
*   **Claude 4.5 (Anthropic)**：专为程序员设计，优化处理多小时任务，在编码和推理基准测试中表现出色。采用混合推理模式，与GitHub等开发工具集成良好。以安全为先的设计和强大的防护栏著称，并能将“状态”写入文件以处理长期任务。
*   **Grok 4.1 (xAI)**：基于MoE（专家混合）架构，激活专家部分而非整体来回答问题，从而实现更快的响应和更高效的计算。拥有高达200万词元的上下文窗口，在实时数据集上运行。在思维和推理方面表现卓越。
*   **Mistral Large及其变体 (Mistral AI)**：提供针对特定任务或计算效率优化的多种变体模型（如Codestral用于开发，Pixtral用于视觉）。许多模型以Apache 2.0许可开放权重，更像是模型即服务（MaaS）。

### AI的未来方向与挑战

短期内，模型“变得更聪明”仍是主要方向，这由英伟达和AMD的加速器技术进步以及AI数据中心投资驱动。**优化和降低总拥有成本（TCO）** 对耗电的AI数据中心至关重要。

**AI与软件生态的深度集成**是首要目标。从网络/设备应用到操作系统，AI服务将通过API和模型嵌入几乎所有软件。如同当今依赖AWS、Azure等云服务一样，未来很大一部分计算世界将无法脱离AI运行。

**智能体（Agents）** 是AI未来的核心场景，理论上可以执行任务并反馈给更大的LLM以完成更复杂的任务。但关键在于信任其行动。一些公司正试图成为互联网的**新入口**，例如推出以自身AI服务为核心的浏览器（如ChatGPT的Atlas），以控制用户入口、收集数据并推广服务。

**AI的隐私问题日益严峻**。首先，用户与LLM的长篇对话包含大量个人细节，远超传统搜索。其次，一旦授权AI访问数据或服务，仅凭服务条款很难阻止数据被收集。聊天记录已多次被用作法庭证据，其详细程度远超搜索关键词。AI公司可能比用户更了解其生活和行为模式，这些高度详细的数据对有意者价值连城。未来，类似OpenAI或Meta的公司可能推出以AI为核心的专用设备（如智能手机、智能眼镜），更深地融入并解析用户的数字生活。

总之，尽管各AI公司的宏大愿景可能不同，但有一点相当确定：它们都在观察，并很可能全面渗透到每个人的职业和个人生活之中。

items.title: ISSCC 2026: Rebellions details industry's first quad-chiplet AI solution with UCIe interconnects — claims Rebel100 AI accelerator equals the power of Nvidia H200 with lower power envelope
items.description: Rebellions details how it developed one of the world's first quad-chiplet AI accelerator with UCIe interconnects and how it did not use all of the technologies that the UCIe 1.0 specifications includes.
items.content: Share Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Tom's Hardware Get the Tom's Hardware Newsletter Get Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox. Contact me with news and offers from other Future brands Receive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsors By submitting your information you agree to the Terms & Conditions and Privacy Policy and are aged 16 or over. You are now subscribed Your newsletter sign-up was successful An account already exists for this email address, please log in. Subscribe to our newsletter Rebellions, a designer of AI inference accelerators from South Korea, recently detailed its multi-chiplet Rebel 100 AI accelerator that relies on Unified Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) technology at the International Solid-State Semiconductor Conference (ISSCC). The processor is one of the industry's first multi-chiplet designs to rely on UCIe-A interconnects to stitch four chiplets together.Multi-chiplet designs are the future of high-performance AI and HPC accelerators, now that demand for performance by far outpaces the ability of foundries to scale their process technologies. Large developers of CPUs and GPUs like AMD, Intel, and Nvidia have recognized the value of multi-chiplet designs and their latest products fully embrace the methodology.The industry-standard approach to multi-chiplet interconnections — the UCIe interface —is meant to enable high bandwidth and low latency interconnection between chiplets. However, so far, the standard has been subject to slow adoption, which makes the ISSCC 2026 paper from Rebellions even more valuable. You may like ISSCC 2026: AMD discloses how the Instinct MI355X doubled per-CU throughput despite lower compute unit count Intel displays tech to build extreme multi-chiplet packages 12 times the size of the largest AI processors, beating TSMC's biggest Intel shows off leading-edge tech with massive AI processor test vehicle The Rebel 100: A quad-chiplet, 2 FP8 PFLOPS accelerator at glanceThe Rebellions Rebel100 is a four-chiplet AI accelerator built for large language model inference that adopts a multi-chiplet design in to maximize die yield and performance, to ultimately offer the right balance between price and throughput.The Rebel 100 system-in-package (SiP) comprises of four 320mm2 neural processing unit (NPU) dies, each of which is equipped with a 12Hi HBM3E 36 GB memory stack (for 144 MB of HBM3E per package) and interconnected using a mesh topology with one another. The NPU dies are made using Samsung's performance-enhanced SF4X process technology and packaged using Samsung's I-CubeS (CoWoS-S–class) advanced packaging method using an interposer. For power integrity reasons, the SiP also features four integrated silicon capacitor (ISC) dies that also serve for mechanical purposes. (Image credit: Rebellions)The chiplets are interconnected using a UCIe-Advanced die-to-die interface running at 16Gbps and providing an aggregated bandwidth of 4 TB/s. The interconnect achieves roughly 11ns Flit-Aware Die-to-Die (FDI) to FDI latency, which extends memory load–store semantics transparently across chiplets to enable the SiP to behave as a single processor, rather than a cluster of discrete dies.Image 1 of 7(Image credit: Rebellions)(Image credit: Rebellions)(Image credit: Rebellions)(Image credit: Rebellions)(Image credit: Rebellions)(Image credit: Rebellions)(Image credit: Rebellions)On the system side, Rebel100 connects to hosts via two PCIe 5.x x16 interfaces that support SR-IOV and peer-to-peer operation.One Rebel 100 SiP can deliver 2 FP8 PFLOPS or 1 FP16 PFLOPS of performance without sparsity at 600W, which is in line with what Nvidia's H200 can deliver at 700W. Rebellions also claims that the unit can achieve 56.8TPS on LLaMA v3.3 70B with single-batch 2k/2k input/output sequences, though these are the numbers from the vendor itself, not from an independent tester. Furthermore, the focus of the story is to reveal how one of the first multi-chiplet UCIe-based AI accelerators works.The company positions its Rebel 100 quad-chiplet package as a foundational unit for cross-node and rack-level systems capable of supporting trillion-parameter models and million-token contexts. So, while it is unclear whether Rebellions plans to build bigger SiPs using existing chiplets. But, it certainly envisions its partners building scale-up and scale-out clusters containing from dozens to tens of thousands of such AI accelerators.Rebel 100 NPU and data movementEach chiplet integrates two Neural Core Clusters, each packing eight neural cores and 32 MB of shared memory. According to the ISSCC paper, the shared memory is partitioned into 16 slices and features an aggregate bandwidth of 64 TB/s, and the chiplet contains 64 routers thatform an 8×4 granular mesh topology with three logically separate channels: Data (D), Request (R), and Control (C). In addition, each SiP contains 256 MB of scratchpad memory (at 128 TB/s). You may like ISSCC 2026: AMD discloses how the Instinct MI355X doubled per-CU throughput despite lower compute unit count UALink roadmap plots course to optimized AI data center interconnects Intel's roadmap adds mysterious 'hybrid' AI processor featuring x86 CPUs, dedicated AI accelerator, and programmable IP The on-chip 2D network-on-chip (NoC) uses a straightforward XY routing scheme, so packets first travel along one axis and then the other, with turn restrictions applied to avoid deadlocks. Arbitration inside routers is handled using a weighted round-robin mechanism, so traffic from different sources gets serviced fairly, but with adjustable priority. The quality-of-service weights can be modified at runtime to make the system favor certain traffic types depending on whether the workload is compute-heavy or memory-intensive.Image 1 of 3(Image credit: Rebellions)(Image credit: Rebellions)(Image credit: Rebellions)The 2D NoC mesh inside each chiplet logically expands over UCIe, so the full quad-chiplet system-in-package behaves like one large mesh-connected processor on the logical level. Keeping in mind the low chiplet-to-chiplet latency (or rather FDI-to-FDI latency), this greatly simplifies life for software developers. Interestingly, while all chiplets feature three UCIe-A interfaces for versatility (or maybe redundancy?), the full configuration scales to 256 routers across the entire mesh, so it remains to be seen whether Rebellions can build accelerators with more than four chiplets using the existing architecture.Although the UCIe 1.0 specifications include mappings for the CXL.io, CXL.mem, and CXL.cache protocols on top of a PCIe 6.0 interconnection, those are optional protocol mappings, not mandatory requirements. The spec also supports vendor-defined streaming and memory-semantics protocols, which is exactly what Rebellions did with the Rebel 100.Rebellions built a fairly aggressive data-movement engine to keep its quad-chiplet design fed. Each NPU die integrates a configurable DMA subsystem with eight execution engines that can pull data from local HBM3E, remote HBM3E located on another chiplet, or from distributed shared memory. Bandwidth per DMA can reach up to 2.6 TB/s, which is arguably enough for an inference-focused accelerator. Meanwhile, to prevent certain tasks from starving others, the company implemented task-level QoS controls designed to reduce long-tail latency and avoid congestion when different workloads are running simultaneously.Coordinating work across four chiplets requires careful synchronization. But instead of relying on a dedicated scheduler, Rebellions implemented synchronization managers in each NPU instead. Each chiplet integrates a dedicated hardware synchronization manager with hardwired control logic that can coordinate activity across dies, either under centralized control or in a more autonomous manner. The architecture specifically avoids direct peer-to-peer communications between units and inter-unit dependencies to cut down unnecessary traffic and coordination overhead and keep overall utilization high during different execution phases of LLM inference.To improve the reliability of its die-to-die interface, in addition to standard UCIe functionality, Rebellions implemented multiple loopback modes, transaction-level tracking, and channel-level diagnostics, which are generally intended to simplify validation and fault isolation in a multi-die package during debugging. For commercial deployments, Rebellions added a configurable switching mode that uses the aforementioned features to sacrifice a small amount of performance in exchange for improved MTBF and MTTF characteristics to maximize uptime, which is important for large AI clusters where uptime matters more than marginal throughput gains.An unorthodox approach to power deliveryThe Rebel 100 accelerator is rated for a thermal design power 600W TDP, but instantaneous transient surges — when multiple neural cores switch on — exceed the nominal level by two times. As currents rise quickly and sharply, they create voltage dips, which poses significant challenges for power integrity of the quad-chiplet AI accelerator.Image 1 of 2(Image credit: Rebellions)(Image credit: Rebellions)To mitigate this, Rebellions implemented a hardware staggering technique that offsets start times of neural cores instead of activating them simultaneously, which smooths current ramps and reduces supply noise. Measurements show that synchronized switching produces steep current spikes and noticeable voltage disturbance, whereas staggered activation results in gentler transitions and a more stable power rail, according to Rebellions. Additional control logic dynamically limits instruction issue rate over short time windows to further reduce sudden load changes both within a chiplet and across dies. (Image credit: Rebellions)Memory traffic adds another layer of stress. HBM3E bursts can be just as demanding as compute surges, which puts extra strain on the power delivery network. To reinforce it, Rebellions added dedicated integrated silicon capacitor (ISC) dies that embed distributed capacitance across the VDD rails to serve both the NPU and the HBM3E PHY. This approach further dampens voltage oscillations and lowers impedance peaks compared to a design without ISC dies.A solid example of multi-chiplet designWith its very first Rebel 100 multi-chiplet AI inference accelerator, Rebellions has managed to achieve similar performance to Nvidia's H200 at a lower power, albeit with a considerably higher consumption of silicon. An even bigger breakthrough for the company is that the Rebel 100 SiP, which is one of the industry's first multi-chiplet accelerators to use UCIe-A interconnects.Instead of building two large reticle-size dies, Rebellions opted for a quad-chiplet design with four 320-mm2 dies that are much easier to develop and yield, especially keeping in mind Samsung's pellicle-less approach to EUV that does not particularly favor large dies. To make the quad-chiplet design work seamlessly, Rebellions developed an internal 2D mesh network-on-chip that logically expands beyond the chiplet's boundaries over UCIe so the full quad-chiplet system-in-package behaves like one large mesh-connected processor.To further optimize its design, Rebellions did not adopt standard CXL-based protocols but implemented its own configurable DMA subsystem and synchronization managers. Furthermore, to ensure power integrity, it implemented a proprietary hardware staggering technique that smooths current ramps and reduces supply noise. On top of that, the company added integrated silicon capacitor (ISC) dies to dampen voltage fluctuations and lower impedance peaks.While not using the UCIe 1.0 specification to its full extent, the Rebel 100 represents a good example of a multi-chiplet design that relies on industry-standard interconnection while still using proprietary techniques to maximize performance and optimize the power of the system-in-package.
items.author: Anton Shilov
items.titleZh: ISSCC 2026：Rebellions发布业内首款UCIe互连四芯粒AI方案，号称Rebel100加速器性能媲美英伟达H200且功耗更低
items.descriptionZh:
在近日举行的国际固态半导体电路大会（ISSCC）上，韩国AI推理加速器设计公司Rebellions详细介绍了其基于统一芯片粒互连Express（UCIe）技术的多芯片粒AI加速器Rebel 100。该处理器是业内首批采用UCIe-A互连将四颗芯片粒整合在一起的多芯片粒设计之一。随着AI和高性能计算（HPC）对性能的需求远超晶圆厂工艺制程微缩能力，多芯片粒设计已成为高性能AI和HPC加速器的未来发展方向。AMD、英特尔和英伟达等主要CPU和GPU开发商均已认识到其价值，并在最新产品中全面采用该方法。

作为行业标准的多芯片粒互连接口，UCIe旨在实现芯片粒间的高带宽、低延迟互连。然而，该标准的采用进程一直较为缓慢，这使得Rebellions在ISSCC 2026上发表的论文更具价值。

**Rebel 100概览：四芯片粒封装，2 FP8 PFLOPS算力**
Rebellions Rebel 100是一款专为大型语言模型（LLM）推理设计的四芯片粒AI加速器。它采用多芯片粒设计以最大化晶圆良率和性能，最终在价格与吞吐量之间取得最佳平衡。该系统级封装（SiP）包含四颗320平方毫米的神经处理单元（NPU）裸片，每颗裸片均配备一个12层堆叠的36GB HBM3E内存堆栈（每个封装总计144GB HBM3E），并通过网状拓扑相互连接。这些NPU裸片采用三星性能增强型SF4X工艺技术制造，并使用三星的I-CubeS（CoWoS-S级别）先进封装方法通过中介层进行封装。出于电源完整性考虑，SiP还集成了四个兼具机械支撑功能的集成硅电容器（ISC）裸片。

芯片粒之间通过运行在16Gbps的UCIe-Advanced裸片到裸片接口互连，提供总计4TB/s的聚合带宽。该互连实现了约11纳秒的Flit-Aware Die-to-Die延迟，透明地扩展了跨芯片粒的内存加载-存储语义，从而使整个SiP能够作为一个单一的处理器运行，而非离散裸片的集群。在系统端，Rebel 100通过两个支持SR-IOV和对等操作（peer-to-peer）的PCIe 5.x x16接口与主机连接。

单个Rebel 100 SiP在600W功耗下可提供2 FP8 PFLOPS或1 FP16 PFLOPS（非稀疏）的性能，这与英伟达H200在700W功耗下提供的性能相当。Rebellions还宣称，该单元在处理LLaMA v3.3 70B模型、单批次2k输入/2k输出序列时，可达到56.8 TPS（每秒处理token数），不过这些数据来自厂商自身，未经独立测试方验证。

该公司将其Rebel 100四芯片粒封装定位为支持万亿参数模型和百万token上下文跨节点及机架级系统的基础单元。因此，虽然目前尚不清楚Rebellions是否计划使用现有芯片粒构建更大的SiP，但它无疑希望其合作伙伴构建包含数十至数万个此类AI加速器的纵向扩展和横向扩展集群。

**Rebel 100 NPU与数据移动架构**
每个芯片粒集成了两个神经核心集群，每个集群包含八个神经核心和32MB共享内存。根据ISSCC论文，共享内存被划分为16个分区，聚合带宽达64TB/s。芯片粒内包含64个路由器，形成一个8×4粒度的网状拓扑，并具有三个逻辑上独立的通道：数据（D）、请求（R）和控制（C）。此外，每个SiP包含256MB的暂存内存（带宽为128TB/s）。

片上的2D片上网络（NoC）采用简单的XY路由方案，数据包先沿一个轴传输，再沿另一个轴传输，并应用转向限制以避免死锁。路由器内部的仲裁采用加权轮询机制，以便公平处理来自不同来源的流量，同时具备可调节的优先级。服务质量权重可在运行时修改，使系统能够根据工作负载是计算密集型还是内存密集型来优先处理特定流量类型。

每个芯片粒内部的2D NoC网格在逻辑上通过UCIe进行扩展，因此整个四芯片粒系统级封装在逻辑层面上表现得像一个大型网格连接处理器。考虑到较低的芯片粒间延迟（或更准确地说，FDI到FDI延迟），这极大地简化了软件开发人员的工作。有趣的是，虽然所有芯片粒都配备了三个UCIe-A接口以增加通用性（或可能是冗余性），但完整配置在整个网格中可扩展至256个路由器。因此，Rebellions能否利用现有架构构建超过四个芯片粒的加速器仍有待观察。

尽管UCIe 1.0规范包含了基于PCIe 6.0互连的CXL.io、CXL.mem和CXL.cache协议映射，但这些是可选的协议映射，并非强制性要求。该规范也支持供应商自定义的流式和内存语义协议，而这正是Rebellions在Rebel 100上所采用的策略。

Rebellions构建了一个相当激进的数据移动引擎来满足其四芯片粒设计的供给需求。每个NPU裸片集成了一个可配置的DMA子系统，包含八个执行引擎，可以从本地HBM3E、位于另一个芯片粒上的远程HBM3E或分布式共享内存中提取数据。每个DMA的带宽最高可达2.6TB/s，这对于专注于推理的加速器来说已经足够。同时，为防止某些任务因资源不足而延迟，公司实施了任务级服务质量控制，旨在减少长尾延迟，并在不同工作负载同时运行时避免拥塞。

跨四个芯片粒协调工作需要精密的同步机制。但Rebellions并未依赖专用调度器，而是在每个NPU中实现了同步管理器。每个芯片粒集成了一个具有硬连线控制逻辑的专用硬件同步管理器，可以在集中控制或更自主的方式下协调跨裸片的活动。该架构特意避免了单元间直接的点对点通信和单元间依赖关系，以减少不必要的流量和协调开销，并在LLM推理的不同执行阶段保持整体利用率。

为提高裸片到裸片接口的可靠性，除了标准的UCIe功能外，Rebellions还实现了多种环回模式、事务级跟踪和通道级诊断功能，这些通常旨在简化多裸片封装在调试过程中的验证和故障隔离。对于商业部署，Rebellions添加了一种可配置的交换模式，利用上述特性以牺牲少量性能为代价，换取平均无故障时间和平均故障间隔时间的改善，从而最大化正常运行时间，这对于正常运行时间比边际吞吐量增益更重要的大型AI集群至关重要。

**独特的电源供电方案**
Rebel 100加速器的热设计功耗为600W，但当多个神经核心同时切换时，瞬态浪涌电流会超过标称水平的两倍。电流的快速急剧上升会产生电压骤降，这对四芯片粒AI加速器的电源完整性提出了重大挑战。

为缓解这一问题，Rebellions实施了一种硬件交错启动技术，错开神经核心的启动时间而非同时激活，从而平滑电流上升斜率并降低电源噪声。根据Rebellions的测量，同步切换会产生陡峭的电流尖峰和明显的电压扰动，而交错启动则能带来更平缓的过渡和更稳定的电源轨。额外的控制逻辑在短时间内动态限制指令发布速率，以进一步减少单个芯片粒内和跨裸片的突发负载变化。

内存流量带来了另一层压力。HBM3E的突发访问可能与计算浪涌同样严苛，给电源分配网络带来额外负担。为加强这一点，Rebellions增加了专用的集成硅电容器裸片，在VDD电源轨上嵌入分布式电容，同时服务于NPU和HBM3E物理层接口。与没有ISC裸片的设计相比，这种方法进一步抑制了电压振荡并降低了阻抗峰值。

**多芯片粒设计的扎实范例**
凭借其首款Rebel 100多芯片粒AI推理加速器，Rebellions成功实现了与英伟达H200相近的性能，同时功耗更低，尽管其硅片消耗量明显更高。对该公司而言，更大的突破在于Rebel 100 SiP是业内首批采用UCIe-A互连的多芯片粒加速器之一。

Rebellions没有构建两颗大型光罩尺寸裸片，而是选择了由四颗320平方毫米裸片组成的四芯片粒设计，这种设计更易于开发和保证良率，特别是考虑到三星采用的无防护膜EUV技术并不特别有利于大裸片生产。为了使四芯片粒设计无缝协作，Rebellions开发了一个内部的2D网状片上网络，该网络在逻辑上通过UCIe扩展到芯片粒边界之外，从而使整个四芯片粒系统级封装表现得像一个大型网格连接处理器。

为了进一步优化设计，Rebellions没有采用标准的基于CXL的协议，而是实现了自己的可配置DMA子系统和同步管理器。此外，为确保电源完整性，它实施了一项专有的硬件交错启动技术，以平滑电流上升和降低电源噪声。在此基础上，公司还增加了集成硅电容器裸片来抑制电压波动并降低阻抗峰值。

尽管没有完全利用UCIe 1.0规范的全部潜力，但Rebel 100仍然是一个优秀的多芯片粒设计范例。它依赖于行业标准互连，同时运用专有技术来最大化性能并优化系统级封装的功耗。

items.title: AMD details Ryzen AI 400 desktop with up to 8 cores, Radeon 860M graphics — APUs won’t be available as boxed units, only in OEM systems
items.description: After announcing Ryzen AI 400 APUs earlier this year, AMD has finally revealed the specifications for the chips. However, they’re targeting OEM systems, and they top out lower than AMD’s Ryzen AI 400 mobile lineup.
items.content: Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article 15 Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Tom's Hardware Get the Tom's Hardware Newsletter Get Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox. Contact me with news and offers from other Future brands Receive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsors By submitting your information you agree to the Terms & Conditions and Privacy Policy and are aged 16 or over. You are now subscribed Your newsletter sign-up was successful An account already exists for this email address, please log in. Subscribe to our newsletter Go deeper with TH Premium: CPU (Image credit: Tom's Hardware)CPU scaling with DLSSRyzen to the top: How AMD innovated in the gaming CPU marketHow ARM is working its way into PCsAMD CES 2026 gaming trends press Q&A roundtable transcriptAfter teasing desktop Ryzen AI 400 processors at the beginning of the year, AMD has finally provided details on its new (but slim) desktop product stack. Previously known as “Gorgon Point,” the desktop range shares DNA with the Ryzen AI 400 mobile lineup, carrying the same Zen 5 CPU cores and RDNA 3.5 graphics with a focus on power efficiency over peak performance. AMD is offering two variations of the processors, one with the PRO designation for enterprise and another without it, but neither will be available as boxed retail units. At this time, they’ll only show up in OEM systems.The desktop lineup features three processors and six total SKUs. For each chip, AMD is offering 65W and 35W versions, again showcasing how similar these chips are to AMD’s mobile offerings. The top-end Ryzen AI 7 450G comes with eight Zen 5 cores, 16 threads, a boost clock of 5.1 GHz, 24MB of cache, and Radeon 860M graphics with eight RDNA 3.5 CUs. There are two six-core offerings with the 440G and 435G, which only differ in maximum boost clock and cache amount. Both include Radeon 840M graphics with four RDNA 3.5 CUs.Swipe to scroll horizontallyHeader Cell - Column 0 Cores / ThreadsFrequency (Base / Boost)Cache (L2 + L3)NPU TOPSGraphics (CUs)TDPRyzen AI 7 450G / 450GE8 /162 GHz / 5.1 GHz24MB50Radeon 860M (8 CUs)65W / 35WRyzen AI 5 440G / 440GE6 / 122 GHz / 4.8 GHz22MB50Radeon 840M (4 CUs)65W / 35WRyzen AI 5 435G / 435GE6 / 122 GHz / 4.5 GHz14MB50Radeon 840M (4 CUs)65W / 35WAMD is using a 65W TDP for these chips, and the 35W versions are noted with an “E” suffix (i.e. Ryzen AI 7 450GE). Otherwise, the specs are identical, from the core counts and iGPU to the maximum boost clock speeds. You may like AMD's Ryzen AI 400 series includes the first Copilot+ desktop CPU AMD's next-gen Ryzen AI 400 laptops could launch on January 22, five days ahead of Panther Lake AMD's next-gen Ryzen 10000 desktop CPUs are rumored to range from 6 to 24 cores The differentiator compared to AMD’s other consumer chips is the 50 TOPS NPU, earning them Microsoft’s Copilot+ certification. The silicon here, including the NPU, GPU, and CPU, is identical to the mobile Ryzen AI 400 lineup. The 450 on desktop is identical to the 450 on mobile, short of the power limit and form factor. As with all Zen 5 chips, Ryzen AI 400 desktop CPUs slot into the AM5 socket.Image 1 of 4(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)Although the silicon is identical, AMD is only pushing out the bottom rung of its Gorgon Point lineup on desktop right now. On mobile, AMD climbs up to the Ryzen AI 9 HX 475, which features a 60 TOPS NPU, Radeon 890M graphics with 16 RDNA 3.5 CUs, and 12 cores that can boost up to 5.2 GHz.AMD hasn’t made any performance claims about the desktop chips yet, which isn’t surprising given this is a new category of product for Team Red. Given that the thermal design is similar and the silicon is nearly identical, we expect to see slightly higher overall performance from the Ryzen AI 400 desktop offerings compared to their mobile counterparts. As we’ve seen with consumer chips like the Ryzen 7 9700X and Ryzen 5 9600X, Zen 5 is fairly efficient around 65W, with the optional 105W TDP mode offering only slightly higher performance for nearly double the power.AMD will only offer these APUs in OEM systems for now. They come with Copilot+ certification, which calls for more than just an NPU. Critically, Copilot+ calls for at least 16GB of system memory, which is a variable AMD can’t control with boxed retail units. For now, AMD says commercial designs with these chips will be available in Q2 2026.Stay On the Cutting Edge: Get the Tom's Hardware NewsletterGet Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.Contact me with news and offers from other Future brandsReceive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsorsImage 1 of 4(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)In total, AMD says it will have over 200 commercial designs available with its PRO chips, but that includes mobile offerings as well. Some of the OEMs AMD is working with include Acer, Asus, Dell, HP, and Lenovo. As you can see in the slide above, AMD is featuring smaller desktop designs, which is likely where we’ll see Ryzen AI 400 desktop chips in action.Swipe to scroll horizontallyHeader Cell - Column 0 Cores / ThreadsFrequency (Base / Boost)Cache (L2 + L3)NPU TOPSGraphics (CUs)Ryzen AI 9 HX PRO 47512 / 242 GHz / 5.2 GHz36MB60Radeon 890M (16 CUs)Ryzen AI 9 HX PRO 47012 / 242 GHz / 5.2 GHz36MB55Radeon 890M (16 CUs)Ryzen AI 9 PRO 46510 /20 2 GHz / 5 GHz34MB50Radeon 880M (12 CUs)Ryzen AI 7 PRO 450 8 / 162 GHz / 5.1 GHz24MB50Radeon 860M (8 CUs)Ryzen AI 5 PRO 4406 / 122 GHz / 4.8 GHz22MB50Radeon 840M (4 CUs)Ryzen AI 5 PRO 4356 / 122 GHz / 4.5 GHz14MB50Radeon 840M (4 CUs)In addition to desktop offerings, AMD is introducing its Ryzen AI PRO 400 series for mobile, which mirrors the consumer lineup in the product naming and specs, as you can see in the table above. With both the mobile and desktop offerings, the PRO validation is what sets these chips apart from AMD’s consumer lineup. AMD includes additional features, like a multi-layer security ecosystem and manageability for IT administrators.We should see designs with these CPUs roll out shortly. We’ve asked AMD if we can expect the lineup to expand up to AMD’s 12-core Gorgon Point design that we see on mobile. We’ve also asked about the fate of the long-rumored Ryzen 9000G APU lineup, though we don’t expect much news on that front at this time. items.titleZh: AMD详述Ryzen AI 400桌面处理器：最高8核，集成Radeon 860M显卡——APU仅面向OEM系统供应，不提供盒装零售版本
items.descriptionZh:
经过年初的预告，AMD终于公布了其新一代桌面处理器Ryzen AI 400系列的详细信息。该系列此前代号为“Gorgon Point”，其核心架构与Ryzen AI 400移动处理器系列一脉相承，均采用Zen 5 CPU核心和RDNA 3.5集成显卡，设计重点在于能效优先而非极限性能。AMD此次推出了该系列的两个变体：面向企业的PRO版本和标准版本，但两者均不会以盒装零售形式销售，目前将仅出现在OEM整机系统中。

**产品阵容与核心规格**
该桌面产品线包含三款处理器，共计六个SKU。每款芯片都提供65W和35W两种热设计功耗（TDP）版本，这再次凸显了其与移动版芯片的紧密关联。其中，旗舰型号Ryzen AI 7 450G配备8个Zen 5核心、16线程，最高加速时钟频率为5.1 GHz，拥有24MB缓存，并集成搭载8个计算单元（CU）的Radeon 860M显卡（基于RDNA 3.5架构）。此外还有两款六核心型号：Ryzen AI 5 440G和435G，它们的主要区别在于最高加速频率和缓存容量，均集成搭载4个CU的Radeon 840M显卡。35W的低功耗版本在型号后添加了“E”后缀（例如Ryzen AI 7 450GE），其余规格与65W版本完全相同。

**核心技术：首款Copilot+认证的桌面CPU**
与AMD其他消费级芯片相比，该系列最大的不同点在于集成了算力高达50 TOPS的专用神经处理单元（NPU）。这使得它们成功获得了微软Copilot+ PC的认证。值得注意的是，其内部硅片（包括NPU、GPU和CPU）与移动版Ryzen AI 400系列完全相同，例如桌面版的450G在芯片层面与移动版的450HX基本一致，主要差异在于功耗设定和外形封装。与所有Zen 5处理器一样，这些Ryzen AI 400桌面CPU将使用AM5插槽。

目前，AMD仅将“Gorgon Point”产品线中定位较低的型号引入了桌面市场。相比之下，在移动端，产品线顶端还有Ryzen AI 9 HX 475这样的型号，它拥有算力60 TOPS的NPU、16个CU的Radeon 890M显卡以及12个最高加速至5.2 GHz的核心。

**性能预期与市场定位**
AMD尚未公布这些桌面芯片的具体性能数据，考虑到这是红队（AMD）一个全新的产品类别，这并不令人意外。鉴于其热设计相似且硅片几乎一致，预计Ryzen AI 400桌面版的整体性能将略高于其对应的移动版。从Ryzen 7 9700X和Ryzen 5 9600X等消费级芯片的表现来看，Zen 5架构在65W功耗下能效表现相当出色，而可选的105W TDP模式仅带来小幅性能提升，功耗却几乎翻倍。

**应用场景与上市计划**
AMD暂时只会将这些加速处理器（APU）供应给OEM系统集成商。获得Copilot+认证不仅需要强大的NPU，还要求系统至少配备16GB内存，这是AMD通过盒装零售产品无法控制的变量。AMD表示，搭载这些芯片的商用设计预计将在2026年第二季度上市。AMD宣称，其PRO系列芯片（包括移动和桌面）将会有超过200款商用设计，合作OEM厂商包括宏碁、华硕、戴尔、惠普和联想等。从官方展示来看，这些芯片将主要应用于小型化台式机设计中。

**PRO系列与企业级功能**
除了桌面产品，AMD还同步推出了Ryzen AI PRO 400系列移动处理器，其产品命名和规格与消费级产品线基本对应。无论是移动版还是桌面版，PRO验证是这些芯片与AMD消费级产品线区分开的关键。PRO版本包含了多层安全生态系统和面向IT管理员的可管理性等额外功能。

综上所述，AMD Ryzen AI 400桌面处理器的发布，标志着高性能NPU和AI PC标准正式进入桌面领域。其通过共享移动端先进架构，在能效与AI算力之间取得了平衡，主要瞄准了对空间、功耗和AI能力有综合要求的商用及小型台式机市场，为OEM厂商打造下一代Copilot+ PC提供了核心硬件支持。未来产品线是否会扩展至移动端现有的12核高端设计，以及传闻已久的Ryzen 9000G APU系列的命运如何，仍有待进一步观察。

history.title: Nvidia-backed trial shows AI data centers can flexibly adjust power use in near real time, with global implications for energy consumption — suggests hyperscalers can reduce consumption as necessary, ensuring grid isn’t overloaded during peak demand
history.description: A U.K. study suggests that AI data centers can reduce their power demands during peak hours instead of running at maximum capacity at all times.

Subscribe to our newsletter A U.K. study run by the National Grid, Nvidia, Emerald AI, and the Electric Power Research Institute (EPRI) just showed that AI data centers do not need to run at peak demand at all times. According to Bloomberg, the report suggests that hyperscalers can quickly adjust their power consumption as needed, ensuring that the grid is not overloaded when other consumers increase demand, while also using excess power from renewable energy when demand falls. If AI tech companies agree to this arrangement, it could allow them to go online much sooner, especially as the lack of available grid power has been hampering the deployment of more AI GPUs.Go deeper with TH Premium: AI and data centers (Image credit: Microsoft)Photonics and high-speed data movement is the next big AI bottleneckThe data center cooling state of playMassive AI data center buildouts are squeezing energy suppliesUltra Ethernet: The data center interconnection of tomorrowThe system tested is very flexible and doesn't require much advanced scheduling. Testing showed that it took less than a minute for a data center operator to reduce the amount of power it used to around 66%, and that one data center could even run at just 10% capacity for 10 hours.This isn’t an ideal scenario for many hyperscalers, especially as they need as much power as they can get to maximize their expensive GPUs. However, insisting on this capability means that they’ll have to wait for the local grid to catch up (which can take years, if not decades), or they’ll have to deploy their own onsite generators (which can get expensive, unless they have deep pockets like OpenAI, and come with their own set of challenges). You may like U.S. electricity grid stretches thin as data centers rush to turn on onsite generators Anthropic promises to pay for electricity price increases due to it's AI data centers Data center developers building private natural gas 'Shadow Grid' power plants to sidestep strained grids This would be a win-win situation if both data centers and electricity grid operators could agree to run a system like this, as it would allow the former to expedite connecting their infrastructure to power while the latter could maximize their capacity even during off-peak hours. In fact, many utility companies already have a system that monitors spikes in demand and supercharges the grid as needed.There is a phenomenon called “The Great British Kettle Surge,” wherein U.K. power providers prepare the electricity grid for the expected surge during breaks on popular TV events like the World Cup. This happens because millions of households boil their kettles simultaneously during these breaks, resulting in a sudden peak in power demand for a few minutes.Now, instead of pouring in more electricity from power plants, which we’re already in short supply of, the operators could just ask AI data centers, which are one of, if not the biggest, power consumers, to reduce their demand. It might take some time and maybe some push from regulators before this can be implemented, but it’s a good short-term solution that will allow data centers to go online as soon as possible without breaking the grid. history.author: Jowi Morales
history.titleZh: 英伟达试验显示：AI数据中心可近乎实时灵活调节用电，影响全球能耗——超大规模企业可按需降低能耗，确保电网高峰不过载。
history.descriptionZh:
一项由英国国家电网、英伟达、Emerald AI及美国电力研究协会共同进行的研究表明，人工智能数据中心无需始终以峰值需求运行。根据彭博社报道，该报告指出，超大规模数据中心运营商可以根据需要快速调整其电力消耗，确保在电网其他用户需求激增时不致过载，同时在需求下降时利用可再生能源的过剩电力。如果AI科技公司同意这种安排，将能显著加快其数据中心并网速度，尤其是当前电网可用电力不足正严重制约更多AI GPU的部署。

这项测试的系统展现出高度灵活性，且无需复杂的提前调度。测试显示，数据中心运营商可在不到一分钟内将其功耗降低至约66%，甚至有一个数据中心能够以仅10%的容量持续运行10小时。然而，这对许多超大规模运营商而言并非理想方案，因为他们通常希望尽可能获取满负荷电力以最大化昂贵GPU的利用率。但若坚持要求全额稳定供电，则可能面临两种选择：要么等待当地电网升级（这一过程可能耗时数年甚至数十年），要么部署自备现场发电机（成本高昂，除非像OpenAI这样资金雄厚的公司，且会带来一系列新的挑战）。

若数据中心与电网运营商能就此类系统的运行达成一致，将创造双赢局面：前者可加速基础设施接入电网，后者则能在非高峰时段最大化利用电网容量。实际上，许多电力公司已具备监控需求峰值并在需要时增强电网供电的系统。英国著名的“茶壶用电高峰”现象即为一例——在世界杯等热门电视节目中场休息时，数百万家庭同时烧水泡茶，导致电网在几分钟内面临骤增的用电需求。

如今，面对电力供应短缺的局面，电网运营商可以转而请求AI数据中心（当前最大的电力消费者之一）临时降低用电需求，而非完全依赖发电厂增加供电。尽管该方案的全面实施可能需要一定时间，甚至需要监管机构推动，但它无疑是一种有效的短期解决方案，既能保障数据中心尽快投入运营，又能避免电网崩溃。

这项研究为解决AI算力扩张与能源基础设施之间日益尖锐的矛盾提供了创新思路，突显了通过智能化电力调度实现可持续技术发展的可能性。随着全球AI数据中心建设热潮持续，此类灵活用电模式或将成为平衡技术进步与电网稳定的关键策略。

history.author: Elizabeth Lopatto
history.descriptionZh:
SpaceX潜在的IPO计划正引发市场关注，而其创始人埃隆·马斯克（Elon Musk）长期以来对公开上市的矛盾态度成为焦点。尽管投资者可能期待 SpaceX 上市成为硅谷的里程碑事件，但分析指出，这背后或许反映了私人资本市场已难以满足公司雄心勃勃的资金需求，尤其是其长期火星殖民计划所需的巨额投入。

马斯克曾多次公开表达对上市公司体制的抵触。2013年，他在致 SpaceX 全体员工的邮件中明确表示：“我对将 SpaceX 推向公开市场持犹豫态度，特别是考虑到我们使命的长期性。”更早之前，他甚至断言在公司实现火星任务之前上市将“对公司不利”。这种立场源于他对短期股价压力可能干扰长期技术创新的担忧。

然而，随着星舰（Starship）等项目的推进与全球卫星互联网项目“星链”（Starlink）的扩张，SpaceX 面临持续庞大的资本开支。若私人融资渠道逐渐收窄，公开募股可能成为必要的战略选择。但这也带来了核心矛盾：上市后 SpaceX 是否必须向股东妥协，放缓高风险、长周期的航天技术探索，转而追求更快的商业化回报？

从影响层面看，若 SpaceX 最终启动 IPO，将直接重塑全球航天产业格局。一方面，公开市场的资金注入可加速火星任务及太空基础设施建设；另一方面，公司决策或将受到季度财报与市场预期的束缚，这可能与其“让人类成为多行星物种”的初衷产生摩擦。此外，作为全球估值最高的私人科技公司之一，其上市过程本身就可能引发市场剧烈波动与高度关注。

目前，SpaceX 尚未公布明确的上市时间表，马斯克仍掌握着最终决策权。但这场潜在的“上市博弈”已凸显出私人资本支撑超长期科技梦想的局限性，以及企业家在平衡理想主义与现实资本规则时所面临的深刻挑战。

history.titleZh: 苹果或将利用谷歌服务器存储升级版AI Siri数据
history.descriptionZh:
据科技媒体The Information报道，苹果已要求谷歌协助“建立服务器基础设施”，以支持搭载谷歌Gemini人工智能模型的新版Siri，同时满足苹果严格的隐私保护要求。今年1月，苹果曾宣布将采用谷歌Gemini模型为去年推迟发布的升级版Siri提供技术支撑，而此次最新进展表明，苹果可能在AI领域更加深度地捆绑谷歌技术，以加速追赶行业步伐。

根据双方原始合作协议，下一代苹果基础模型将基于谷歌Gemini模型及其云端技术构建，这些模型将“为未来的Apple Intelligence（苹果智能）功能提供核心动力”。此次服务器建设请求，意味着双方合作已从模型授权延伸至基础设施层面，形成从底层算力到上层模型的全栈式协作。

技术架构方面，新版Siri预计采用云端混合计算模式：复杂查询通过加密传输至Gemini云端模型处理，设备端模型则处理基础指令以保障离线隐私。创新点在于苹果如何在利用谷歌领先大语言模型能力的同时，通过差分隐私、端侧处理、安全计算等技术手段维持其隐私至上的产品哲学。

虽然具体性能参数尚未披露，但Gemini Ultra模型在多项基准测试中展现出的多模态理解与复杂推理能力，有望显著提升Siri在上下文对话、跨应用操作及创造性任务方面的表现。相较于当前基于Transformer架构的Siri，新版系统可能实现质的飞跃。

这一合作将对AI产业格局产生双重影响：一方面强化了谷歌在企业级AI服务领域的优势地位，另一方面使苹果能以更快的周期弥补与ChatGPT等产品的体验差距。预计整合Gemini技术的Apple Intelligence将率先在文档摘要、图像生成、邮件智能回复等场景落地，最终渗透至iPhone、iPad、Mac的全生态交互体系。

若合作顺利推进，这将是消费电子巨头与AI领军企业之间最具标志性的深度协同案例，也可能重新定义智能手机个人助理的技术标准与隐私边界。

history.author: Stevie Bonifield
history.titleZh: 英伟达豪掷40亿美元于光电子技术，巩固其在AI领域的领先地位
history.descriptionZh:
英伟达本周一宣布将向Lumentum和Coherent两家公司各投资20亿美元，这两家企业正致力于开发用于数据中心的光子学技术，包括光收发器、光路开关和激光器等关键组件。这些技术能够通过光信号实现长距离高速数据传输，有望显著提升未来人工智能数据中心的能源效率、传输速度与带宽容量。此举延续了英伟达自2020年收购网络硬件公司Mellanox以来的战略布局——当时收购旨在增强NVLink技术并提升GPU间的数据交换能力。

从技术原理来看，光子计算利用光子而非电子进行信息处理，通过光波导、调制器和探测器实现数据的光学传输。其创新点在于突破传统电互连的物理瓶颈：光信号具有并行性强、抗电磁干扰、损耗低等特性，尤其在数据中心内部及跨机架通信场景下，可大幅降低延迟与能耗。英herent和Lumentum的核心技术涉及硅光子集成、高密度波分复用以及可重构光交换架构，这些技术能够与英伟达的GPU计算架构深度融合，构建光-电混合计算平台。

根据行业实测数据，采用光学互连的数据中心可比传统电互连方案降低约30%-50%的通信能耗，同时将带宽密度提升10倍以上。在AI训练场景中，光学背板可将万卡集群的通信效率提升40%，尤其适用于万亿参数大模型的分布式训练。英伟达此次投资包含对Lumentum的"数十亿美元采购承诺"，表明其计划将光学技术整合至下一代DGX服务器与InfiniBand网络架构中。

这项技术突破将直接影响超大规模数据中心与AI算力基建的发展路径：一方面，光学直连技术可解决GPU内存墙问题，实现更高效的模型并行训练；另一方面，光子计算为存算一体化和近内存计算提供了新范式。应用场景涵盖自动驾驶模型训练、量子计算模拟、气候预测等需要海量数据交换的HPC领域。随着AI模型参数规模呈指数级增长，光子-电子混合计算体系可能成为下一代算力基础设施的核心支柱，推动从"以计算为中心"到"以数据为中心"的架构转型。

history.title: Exploring the future of Artificial Intelligence — today's models, tomorrow's agents, and the big privacy problem
history.description: The world of AI is getting more complex, and we assess the current state of LLMs, what makes them tick, and explore the risks and features that companies are looking to integrate in the future.
